wikitext:
{{Chess Position|Centre pawn opening |eco=[[Chess/ECOC|C20]] |parent=[[../|Open game]] }} == 2. c3?! · Centre pawn opening == White wishes to build a big, two-pawn centre, and plays 2. c3?! to support pushing the pawn to d4. This idea, c3 to support d4, is a common plan for White in many open games (games beginning [[../|1. e4 e5]]): c3 after developing the knight is the Ponziani ([[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. Nf3/2...Nc6/3. c3|2. Nf3 Nc6 3. c3]]), and it comes after developing the knight and bishop in the Italian and Spanish games ([[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. Nf3/2...Nc6/3. Bc4/3...Bc5/4. c3|3. Bc4 Bc5 4. c3]] or e.g. [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. Nf3/2...Nc6/3. Bb5/3...Bc5/4. c3|3. Bb5 Bc5 4. c3]]3...Bc5 is a sideline today, the classical defence. The same idea occurs in the main line later: 3. Bb5 a6 4. Ba4 Nf6 5. O-O Be7 6. Re1 b5 7. Bb3 d6 8. c3, but the line is longer and makes for a worse illustration of the point.). However, played on White's second move without any pieces developed, it is simply too slow. Black can respond vigorously with '''2...d5''' to eliminate transpositional possibilities. After 3. exd5 Qxd5{{chess/not|-}}, there is no 4. Nc3 to chase the queen away and gain a tempo as the c3 pawn is occupying the square. Staunton suggested that by avoiding taking the pawn, White could keep the game competitive: 3. Nf3 dxe4 4. Nxe5 Bd6!? 5. Nc4 Be6 6. d4 exd3 e.p. 7. Bxd3{{chess/not|+}}. However, 4...Qe7! is an improvement, which forces White to move the knight again and gums up White's play for the centre, as d4 will be met with exd3 e.p. and a discovered check. Black sweeps ahead in development to a reach a dominating position. 5. Nc4 Nc6 6. Be2 Bf5 7. O-O O-O-O{{chess/not|-}}. === History === {{Chess/board |moves=1. e4 e5 2. c3 Nf6 3. Qc2 Bc5 4. Nf3 Nc6 5. Bb5 d6 6. d4 exd4 7. cxd4 Bb4+ 8. Nc3 Bd7 9. O-O |caption=Position given by Ruy López. |frame=1 |float=right}} 2. c3?! is very unusual today but has a long history. [[w:Howard Staunton|Staunton]] analysed the opening in his 1847 book{{Cite book |title=The Chess-Player's Handbook |last=Staunton |first=Howard |publisher=Henry G. Bohn |year=1847 |location=London |pages=238-243 |url=https://archive.org/details/chessplayershan00staugoog}}, writing that it was known under the descriptive title "'''the queen's bishop's pawn opening'''" in England. In his chapter on this line he offers a survey of previous analyses and notes,
This mode of commencing the battle was probably a favourite in the early days of European chess play, as Ruy Lopez has devoted more than ordinary space and attention to it in his often-quoted work of the sixteenth century.
It is the first opening covered in [[w:Ruy López de Segura|Ruy López de Segura]]'s 1561 treatise, where he gives the line 2...Nf6 3. Qc2 Bc5 4. Nf3 Nc6 (López gives 4...Qe7 5. d4?? exd4 with threat of ...Qxe4 as an improvement for Black) 5. Bb5 d6 (a mistake, given it pins the knight) 6. d4 exd4 7. cxd4 Bb4+ 8. Nc3 Bd7 9. O-O{{chess/not|++}}.{{Cite book |title=The Art of the Game of Chess |last=López |first=Ruy |publisher=The Catholic University of America Press |year=2020 |location=Washington D.C. |pages=119-120 |orig-date=1561 |translator-last1=McGrath |translator-first1=Michael J.}} López reckoned without the reply 2...d5, which, Staunton notes, was considered to be the best retort by the time of [[w:François-André Danican Philidor|Philidor]] and [[w:Johann Baptist Allgaier|Allgaier]]. Eric Schiller re-christened 2. c3 after '''MacLeod Call''', who "was not a very good player, but he was persistent in his use of this opening. At the 6th American Chess Congress of 1889, he relied on his pet opening, but finished at the bottom of the crosstable."{{Cite book |title=Unorthodox Chess Openings |last=Schiller |first=Eric |publisher=Cardoza Publishing |year=1998 |pages=239}} In recent years some databases have begun to call this, confusingly, the "'''Lopez opening'''". Unlikely to be in reference to Ruy Lopez's book (from which 1. e4 e5 2. Nf3 Nc6 3. Bb5 is the better known line, which more usually bears his name), the misunderstanding may have arisen from [https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Talk:Centre_Pawn_Opening&oldid=1101331183#Requested_move_22_July_2022 a longstanding editorial gaffe at Wikipedia]. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1. e4 e5 2. c3'''
